skill.name: using-xtool
skill.description: Guides setup and use of xtool for Xcode-free iOS development with SwiftPM, including project structure, xtool.yml configuration, app extensions, device workflows, and common troubleshooting.
skill.summary: Build and configure SwiftPM iOS apps with xtool without Xcode project files.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many code execution patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, inline command names, and configuration snippets rather than executable skill code. The hardcoded URLs and key-related detections are plist examples and extension identifiers, with no evidence of network exfiltration, credential theft, prompt injection, or obfuscation.

low_findings.title: External Command Guidance Requires User Oversight
low_findings.description: The skill documents xtool commands for project creation, builds, device deployment, authentication, certificates, and profiles. These are legitimate for the stated iOS development purpose, but users should review commands before execution because they affect local projects and Apple developer state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly document external CLI commands. The risk is low because the skill is instructional Markdown and does not contain scripts that automatically execute commands.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples for file names, commands, Swift, YAML, XML, and project structure. No executable Ruby code, shell interpolation, or command injection path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown guide and the flagged locations are visible code examples or inline literals. There is no source file, script entry point, or dynamic execution primitive present.

low_findings.title: Network and Credential Static Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL detections are Apple plist DTD declarations in XML examples. The certificate, key, and weak cryptography detections come from normal iOS extension identifiers and SwiftPM metadata, not from credential access or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL strings are standard plist DTD references and no network client is present. The key-related text is an Apple extension identifier, not a file read or secret handling operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Dangerous Combination Heuristic
dangerous_patterns.description: The heuristic combined command, network, and credential indicators, but manual review found no executable code, no outbound request implementation, and no credential collection logic. No prompt injection attempt was found in the skill text.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The full file is a Markdown usage guide with examples only. The suspicious combination is explained by documentation for xtool, Apple plist XML, and iOS extension identifiers.

content.value_statement: Cross-platform iOS development often fails when teams mix xtool with XcodeGen or Xcode project assumptions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for SwiftPM projects, xtool.yml files, extensions, and device workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains the correct xtool project structure using Package.swift, xtool.yml, and Sources directories.
Provides command references for creating, building, running, installing, launching, and uninstalling iOS apps with xtool.
Shows minimal and expanded xtool.yml configuration for bundle IDs, products, resources, icons, entitlements, and extensions.
Guides creation of WidgetKit and other app extension targets with SwiftPM products and Info.plist entries.
Documents common xtool mistakes and troubleshooting steps for devices, authentication, SDK setup, and first builds.
limitations: Does not install xtool, Apple SDKs, or developer certificates for the user.
Does not replace Apple account access, device trust steps, or provisioning requirements.
Focuses on xtool workflows and may not apply to XcodeGen, Tuist, or native Xcode project files.
Provides examples that users must adapt to their bundle IDs, targets, app icons, resources, and entitlements.

use_cases.title: Create a New xtool App
use_cases.description: Set up a SwiftPM iOS app with the expected xtool file layout and a minimal xtool.yml configuration.

use_cases.title: Add an App Extension
use_cases.description: Add a WidgetKit, share, action, Safari, keyboard, or Today extension using SwiftPM products and extension plist settings.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile engineer extending an existing app

use_cases.title: Debug Build and Device Issues
use_cases.description: Resolve common xtool mistakes around commands, connected devices, authentication, SDK setup, and first-run builds.

prompt_templates.title: Start an xtool Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new xtool iOS app named MyApp. Show the expected files, the minimal xtool.yml, and the first commands to run.

prompt_templates.title: Review xtool Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my xtool.yml plan for an iOS app with resources, an icon, entitlements, and a custom Info.plist. Point out missing or incorrect fields.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Widget Extension
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through adding a WidgetKit extension to an existing xtool SwiftPM app. Include Package.swift changes, xtool.yml changes, and the required Info.plist entries.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose xtool Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an xtool app that builds but will not run on my device. Help me inspect likely issues with devices, Developer Mode, Apple authentication, provisioning profiles, and command usage.

output_examples.input: I want to add a WidgetKit extension to my xtool app.
output: A checklist of Package.swift product and target changes.
The xtool.yml extension entry that points to the widget Info.plist.
A minimal explanation of the widget extension identifier and build command.

output_examples.input: My app fails because I used xtool build.
output: A correction that xtool dev build is the expected build command.
A short explanation of why xtool dev is the main build and run workflow.
Next checks for SDK setup, connected devices, and authentication state.

output_examples.input: I have an XcodeGen project.yml and want xtool support.
output: A warning that xtool uses xtool.yml and SwiftPM instead of project.yml.
A migration outline for Package.swift, Sources directories, bundle IDs, and resources.
A list of items that require project-specific adaptation.

best_practices: Use SwiftPM products and targets as the source of truth for app and extension modules.
Keep xtool.yml focused on bundle ID, product, resources, icon, entitlements, and extension mappings.
Review xtool commands before running them, especially authentication, certificate, profile, install, and uninstall commands.
anti_patterns: Do not use XcodeGen project.yml or Tuist Project.swift files as xtool configuration.
Do not place extension code in an Extensions directory when xtool expects SwiftPM targets under Sources.
Do not assume xtool build, xtool run, or xtool generate are the main commands when xtool dev subcommands are required.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants guide xtool-based iOS development with SwiftPM, xtool.yml, app extensions, and device workflows.

faq.question: Does this skill run xtool commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide. Users should review and approve any suggested terminal commands.

faq.question: Does xtool use XcodeGen project.yml files?
faq.answer: No. The skill explains that xtool uses xtool.yml and Package.swift, not project.yml or Project.swift files.

faq.question: Can it help add WidgetKit extensions?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes the SwiftPM target, product, xtool.yml extension entry, Info.plist identifier, and sample widget structure.

faq.question: Can it solve Apple authentication or provisioning by itself?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest xtool commands and checks, but Apple account access and device trust steps remain user controlled.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked low?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation only, but it guides external xtool commands that can affect local projects, devices, and Apple developer state.
